On Thursday morning, security lines at Atlanta briefly reached 90 minutes, while wait times at Houston’s George Bush topped three hours.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) said at least 366 security officers have quit since the start of the partial government shutdown, including more than 30 officers across New England, The Boston Globe reported.

On Tuesday, four airports—New York’s John F. Kennedy, Atlanta’s Hartsfield-Jackson, Houston’s Hobby and New Orleans’—saw more than one third of TSA workers call out sick.

How To Monitor Wait Times At Your Airport

The rate of TSA callouts is different at every airport, making it hard for travelers to know how early to head to the airport. Unfortunately, the MyTSA app, which normally monitors wait times at airports, is “not being actively managed” due to the TSA funding lapse caused by the DHS shutdown, according to the TSA website. Travelers’ best bets are checking the website of the departure airport, which will likely display wait times by terminal, as well as their airline app, which may provide information on wait times.

Why Are Tsa Staff Working Without Pay?

TSA workers are classified as “excepted” federal workers, which means they must work without pay during a funding lapse. While the average salary for TSA officers is $51,040, entry-level salaries begin at $40,270, according to Bureau of Labor Statistics data. Most screeners are “living paycheck to paycheck,” one TSA officer told Forbes, adding TSA is “disciplining officers for not having enough money to buy gas or groceries.” Another officer said TSA is asking for documentation from officers who put in for personal time—including financial documentation “that explains the hardship you are experiencing.”

Is Flying Less Safe During The Partial Shutdown?

DHS officials have insisted it is safe to fly, but two TSA officers suggested to Forbes that much longer lines at airports are a security threat. “Closing checkpoints is adding to the risk,” one said. Longer lines increase risk because now “thousands of passengers have not been screened so we don’t know what’s in their bags.”

Further Reading

One Third Of TSA Workers At 4 Airports Called Out Sick Tuesday (Forbes)

item.title: Republicans Might Break With Trump Over Iran War Funding
item.description: The war reportedly cost more than $11 billion in the first week alone.
item.dc:creator: Sara Dorn, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

Several Republicans are hesitant to support the White House’s incoming request for billions of dollars more in funding for the Iran war—another sign the conflict is creating political tensions for President Donald Trump.

Key Facts

Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth acknowledged Thursday the Pentagon would request additional funding for the war, all but confirming a report in The Washington Post a day earlier that the Defense Department will seek $200 billion from Congress.

Some Republicans seemed uneasy about the request.

At least one, Rep. Lauren Boebert, R-Colo., said she was against additional funding for the Iran war, telling CNN, “I am a no . . . I am so tired of spending money over there,” adding, “I have folks in Colorado who can’t afford to live. We need America first policies right now.”

Boebert’s take represents a viewpoint that has already been bubbling up among some prominent figures in Trump’s base, including former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene, R-Ga., and Tucker Carlson, who accuse Trump of abandoning his “America First” policies and promise of “no new wars” during his 2024 campaign.

Other Republicans, including Reps. Tom Massie, R-Ky. and Chip Roy, R-Texas, and Sen. Lisa Murkowski, R-Alaska, said they won’t back the request until the Trump administration clearly explains its goals, objectives and timeline for the war to Congress.

Congress can’t approve the request without near-majority support from members under Republicans’ slim majority in the House.

Tangent

Neither House Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., nor Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., agreed to support the funding request when prompted by reporters Thursday. “I’m sure it’s not a random number, so we’ll look at that,” Johnson said, adding, “it’s a dangerous time in the world, and we have to adequately fund defense.” Thune acknowledged “the operations that are being undertaken now in Iran cost money,” adding, “we have to recognize that.”

Crucial Quote

“We want to be in the best shape, the best shape we’ve ever been in,” Trump told reporters when asked about the funding request, adding, “It’s a small price to pay to make sure that we stay tippy top.”

Key Background

The Pentagon has reportedly asked the White House to request the additional $200 billion from Congress in the coming days, indicating the military campaign is far from over. The cost of the war—more than $11 billion in the first six days alone, Pentagon officials reportedly told lawmakers—has already raised concerns among some Republicans. “It’s considerably higher than I would have guessed, but I don’t know how it’s broken down,” Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine, told reporters Wednesday. Hegseth said Thursday the figure “could move,” adding, “it takes money to kill bad guys.” He would not say how long the war could last and how the U.S. would decide it’s over, reiterating the decision is Trump’s.

Further Reading

‘It Takes Money To Kill Bad Guys’: Hegseth Doesn’t Deny Pentagon’s $200 Billion Iran War Funding Request (Forbes)

item.title: ABC Pulls ‘The Bachelorette’ After Taylor Frankie Paul Alleged Assault Video
item.description: A spokesperson for Paul told Forbes the video footage, published by TMZ Thursday, “omits context” and was released to “harm Taylor without any regard for the consequences for their child.”
item.dc:creator: Conor Murray, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

ABC pulled the upcoming season of “The Bachelorette” amid growing controversy over domestic violence allegations against Taylor Frankie Paul, which escalated Thursday after TMZ published video footage of Paul throwing chairs at her ex, Dakota Mortensen, in the presence of her daughter.

Key Facts

A Disney Entertainment Television spokesperson told multiple outlets, “In light of the newly released video just surfaced today, we have made the decision to not move forward with the new season of ‘The Bachelorette’ at this time, and our focus is on supporting the family.”

The decision came shortly after TMZ published the video footage, released just three days before Paul’s season of “The Bachelorette” was slated to premiere on ABC, amid growing controversy as Paul and Mortensen face a separate domestic assault investigation in Utah.

The video depicts a 2023 altercation between Paul and Mortensen that resulted in Paul being charged with felony aggravated assault and domestic violence.

A spokesperson for Paul told Forbes the video was released as part of a “never-ending, desperate, attention-seeking, destructive campaign to harm Taylor without any regard for the consequences for their child,” saying it is an “old video” that “conveniently omits context.”

What Happens In The Video Of Paul?

The video depicts Paul and Mortensen in a heated argument, with Paul at one point kicking Mortensen before grabbing barstool chairs and throwing them at him. After Paul throws the first chair, Mortensen says, “Your daughter is right here,” before Paul throws a second and third chair. Mortensen is later heard saying, “Your daughter just got hit in the head with a metal chair.” Paul’s daughter is heard crying throughout the video, as Paul yells at Mortensen, “You did this,” and, “Get away from my daughter.”

What Happened After The 2023 Incident?

Paul was arrested by Herriman City, Utah, police on three misdemeanor charges of assault, criminal mischief and domestic violence in the presence of a child in February 2023. A release by the police department said there was video evidence suggesting Paul’s “minor child was injured by an action of Ms. Paul.” The Salt Lake City District Attorney’s Office charged Paul with one felony count of aggravated assault and two felony counts of domestic violence in the presence of a child, though Paul claimed in an appearance on the “Call Her Daddy” podcast last year that the charges were dropped and that she never hurt her daughter.

Key Background

Paul has been the subject of growing controversy after the Draper, Utah, police department confirmed the ongoing domestic assault investigation to People earlier this week. Details of the investigation are unclear, but police said allegations have been made on both sides. The investigation prompted “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives” to pause production on its fifth season, and ABC sponsor Cinnabon cut ties with both “Mormon Wives” and “The Bachelorette.” Paul addressed the investigation in an appearance on “Good Morning America” on Wednesday morning, saying it is “a heavy time” and that the incident being “broadcasted out there in these headlines” feels “like the end of the world.”

Further Reading

‘Bachelorette’ Taylor Frankie Paul Says Domestic Violence Investigation Feels Like ‘The End Of The World’ (Forbes)

item.title: Kanye West Settles Another Copyright Lawsuit Days Before New Album Drops
item.description: West has faced a dozen copyright lawsuits over the past several years accusing him of sampling music without permission.
item.dc:creator: Conor Murray, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

Kanye West reached a settlement agreement in a lawsuit filed by Memphis rappers who accused him of “blatant” copyright infringement for sampling their song without permission, days before West is slated to drop his new album.

Key Facts

West reached a settlement agreement with rappers Criminal Manne, DJ Squeeky and the estate of the late rapper Kilo G, according to documents filed in Tennessee federal court.

A court filing made on Wednesday says the parties have “reached a settlement agreement in principle resolving all claims,” and a filing by district judge Thomas L. Parker on Thursday granted the parties a 30-day period to submit a final written settlement.

The rappers accused West and Ty Dolla $ign of “brazen” copyright infringement by using a sample of their song, “Drink a Yak (Part 2),” without permission on “Fuk Sumn,” a song West and Ty Dolla $ign released in 2024.

The complaint, filed in November 2024, accused representatives for West and Ty Dolla $ign of reaching out to clear the sample only after “Fuk Sumn” was released, and the complaint alleges negotiations broke down months later because West fired his entire legal and business team.

Ty Dolla $ign reached a settlement with the plaintiffs in July 2025, though West remained a defendant.

Forbes has reached out to lawyers representing both West and the plaintiffs for comment.

What Do We Know About Kanye West’s Upcoming Album?

West’s new album “Bully” is scheduled to drop on March 27, though the album’s release date has been postponed multiple times over the past year. “Bully” was initially slated to release last summer, before setting release dates in December and then January. West promoted songs from “Bully” at a concert in Shanghai in July, which was slammed by The Hollywood Reporter as “chaotic” as fans reportedly chanted for refunds after the rapper showed up 40 minutes late, leaving attendees waiting in heavy rain. West also set his first U.S. concert dates in years, booking SoFi Stadium in Los Angeles for two dates in April. Variety previously reported “Bully” contains samples from artists including the Carpenters and the Supremes, noting it is unclear whether West secured permission from these artists to sample their music.

Who Else Has Accused West Of Copyright Infringement?

Some of West’s latest music releases sparked accusations and lawsuits accusing the rapper of sampling copyrighted music without permission, including a high-profile lawsuit by Donna Summer’s estate, which was settled in May 2024. The suit had accused West of sampling Summer’s classic hit “I Feel Love” on his song, “Good (Don’t Die),” after her estate “explicitly denied” permission for him to do so. “Good (Don’t Die)” is no longer available on streaming services. West has been sued 12 times for copyright since 2019, Billboard reported. The most recent suit was filed last month by Swedish artist SWSH, who accused West and Ty Dolla $ign of sampling her 2018 song “Break the Fall” without permission.

Tangent

West was found liable by a jury and ordered by a California court last week to pay $140,000 after a former project manager who worked on West’s $57 million Malibu home alleged he was injured while working and wrongfully terminated. West filed a motion earlier this week to overturn the jury’s decision.

Key Background

West previously ranked on the Forbes billionaire list thanks to a partnership with Adidas, through which he sold his popular Yeezy shoes. West lost the Yeezy partnership—and with it, much of his net worth—after the brand cut ties following a series of antisemitic and bizarre remarks. West continued to make antisemitic remarks for years, including selling swastika shirts and posting, “IM A NAZI,” in 2025. West apologized for his antisemitic behavior in a full-page Wall Street Journal ad in January, blaming a brain injury that caused “serious damage to my mental health and led to my bipolar type-1 diagnosis.”

Further Reading

Kanye West Blames Brain Injury For Years Of Antisemitic Rhetoric In WSJ Ad (Forbes)

Kanye West Sets March Release Date for ‘Bully’ Album (Rolling Stone)

item.title: Qatar Under Fire: Complains Of Lost Energy Revenue—Billionaire Warns Iran Of Expanding ‘Mad War’
item.description: World leaders called on Iran to end strikes on energy sites.
item.dc:creator: Ty Roush, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

Qatar reportedly lost about $20 billion in annual revenue after Iranian strikes crippled the country’s liquefied natural gas export capacity, as billionaire and former Qatari Prime Minister Hamad bin Jassim bin Jaber Al Thani warned Iran’s attacks only served Israel’s interests and risked dragging the whole region into a “mad war.”

Key Facts

Saad al-Kaabi, QatarEnergy’s chief executive, told Reuters on Thursday about 17% of Qatar’s LNG export capacity was knocked out by Iranian strikes, causing an estimated $20 billion in lost annual revenue.

Repairs to Qatar’s facilities—providing resources to ExxonMobile, Shell and other global energy firms—will cut about 12.8 million tons per year of LNG for up to five years, al-Kaabi said.

Hours after Iran struck Qatari natural gas sites on Wednesday in response to Israel attacking South Pars, the Iranian gas field shared with Qatar, Al Thani wrote on X: “We have never been your enemies, but what you are doing today is a rejected and unjustified act.”

Al Thani said Iran’s attack on Qatari sites “serves” and “achieves” Israel’s goals and threatens to push the “region toward the abyss of a mad war.”

Tangent

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer issued a joint statement with his counterparts from France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands and Japan on Thursday, calling for Iran to cease attacks on energy sites and in the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of the global oil supply flows. The leaders said interference with international shipping and the disruption of global energy supply chains “constitute a threat to international peace and security.”

Forbes Valuation

Al Thani has an estimated net worth of $4.5 billion as of Thursday, ranking him the 912th-richest person in the world. He served as Qatar’s prime minister from 2007 to 2013 and as foreign minister from 1992 until 2013. Al Thani owns about 4.5% of Deutsche Bank through Paramount Services Holdings, his largest publicly traded holding.

Key Background

Since the U.S. and Israel launched attacks last month, Iran has retaliated by striking U.S. allies across Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, the United Arab Emirates and others. These strikes have targeted key energy infrastructure, leading to concerns of a disruption to the global energy market. President Donald Trump said late Wednesday the U.S. was unaware of Israel’s plans to strike the South Pars gas field and said no additional attacks would happen. Trump warned Iran that, should the country continue attacking a “very innocent” Qatar’s natural gas facilities, the U.S. would “massively blow up” the entirety of the South Pars gas field “at an amount of strength and power that Iran has never seen or witnessed before.”

Further Reading

MORE FROM FORBESTrump Threatens To ‘Blow Up’ Iran’s Biggest Gas Field If It Continues Striking QatarBy Siladitya Ray

item.title: Trump Says ‘We’re Not Putting Troops Anywhere’ As Iran War Nears 3-Week Mark
item.description: The Pentagon is seeking $200 billion in additional funding for the Iran war, which has continued for nearly three weeks.
item.dc:creator: Antonio Pequeño IV, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

The U.S. does not plan to deploy troops on the ground in Iran, at least for now, according to a statement President Donald Trump made Thursday, shooting down the use of boots on the ground as the war with Iran nears its third week and the Pentagon seeks additional funding for the conflict.

Key Facts

Trump told reporters, “We’re not putting troops anywhere,” when asked about the recent deployment of a Marine expeditionary unit to the Middle East.

The unit includes 2,200 Marines aboard three U.S. Navy amphibious ships, which hold F-35B Lightning II jets and MV-22B Ospreys.

Trump’s comments came after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth did not deny the Pentagon is requesting an additional $200 billion for the war in Iran, which Trump called “a small price to pay to make sure that we stay tippy top.”

Contra

“If I were [putting boots on the ground] I certainly wouldn’t tell you,” Trump added before boasting about the Dow Jones Industrial average reaching the 50,000 point mark in early February (the index is down 7.3% in the last month of trading to about 45,800).

Big Number

$11.3 billion. That is approximately how much the war in Iran cost the U.S. in the first week alone, according to multiple outlets that cited a closed-door briefing involving Pentagon officials.

Key Background

Trump has repeatedly said there are no imminent plans to place boots on the ground since the war against Iran began at the end of February, though the president has not ruled out ground deployments, with Sen. Richard Blumenthal, D-Conn., telling reporters shortly after the conflict began he was “more fearful than ever” about boots on the ground. Trump is seeking a decisive victory using aerial strikes as opposed to a drawn out occupation in Iran, though he said last week the war will be “as long as it's necessary,” seemingly backing out from the four to five week timeline he initially estimated at the start of the conflict. Over 50,000 U.S. troops are in the Middle East, according to The New York Times, with the U.S. death toll reaching at least 13.

Further Reading

‘It Takes Money To Kill Bad Guys’: Hegseth Doesn’t Deny Pentagon’s $200 Billion Iran War Funding Request (Forbes)

U.S. Deploying Thousands More—Marines And 3 Warships—In Iran War Ramp Up (Forbes)

item.title: Your Biggest Cyber Risk Isn’t What You Think
item.description: Also in the Forbes CIO newsletter: Nvidia’s full stack AI approach, NanoClaw adds security to a powerful AI agent, researchers try to “teach” AI robots.
item.dc:creator: Megan Poinski, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Technology and the stock market seem to operate in separate universes. Looking at the stock market this week, one might think it’s a bad time for tech. Market indexes are all down, and tech is providing no relief. Nvidia’s stock is down 2.9% this week, and Microsoft, Google, Meta and Amazon are all trading at lower levels now than when markets opened on Monday.

The stock market masks the fact that Nvidia just projected $1 trillion in annual revenue this year—a development that one would think would generate excitement among investors in all kinds of tech, considering that Nvidia’s primary product is the infrastructure underlying most AI. And even without the ongoing war in Iran, the skyrocketing gas prices it brings, and dispiriting economic indicators, including a freeze in interest rates, tech stocks would likely still be relatively down. Wall Street tends to second-guess AI companies’ revenues and projections, questioning whether developments will translate into real increases in business. In Nvidia’s case, it’s consistently outperformed expectations in the recent past—though as the year progresses, we’ll see if it’s set the bar too high.

One thing that impacts every company in every sector is cybersecurity. Remedio CEO Tal Kollender says many companies are overlooking significant risks by focusing on weaknesses in big systems—but forgetting about smaller vulnerabilities across systems, applications and AI. I spoke with her about how to find these weak points and shore them up. An excerpt from our conversation is later in this newsletter.

This is the published version of Forbes’ CIO newsletter, which offers the latest news for chief innovation officers and other technology-focused leaders. Click here to get it delivered to your inbox every Thursday.


FROM THE HEADLINES

Nvidia’s GTC 2026 wraps up today, and it’s been a week full of announcements about the next chapter of AI from the company that’s synonymous with its infrastructure. But the biggest announcement wasn’t truly about new products. At his keynote address, CEO Jensen Huang unveiled the next step for Nvidia: A five-layered full-stack platform approach to AI, showing that the company will own every section of the AI stack, writes Forbes senior contributor Janakiram MSV.

At the bottom is Nvidia’s compute substrate, anchored by its Vera Rubin platform with its seven specialized chips, five rack-scale systems and an integrated supercomputer built for AI workloads. Above that is the networking and data acceleration layer, with NVLink 6 and Spectrum-X. The agent runtime layer is in the middle, represented by the new enterprise NemoClaw stack. Next is the open model ecosystem through the Nemotron Coalition, covering six frontier model families. At the top is the AI factory design layer, with the DSX reference architecture and Omniverse Digital Twin blueprint.

MSV writes that this is the logical next step for Nvidia. The world’s most valuable company became dominant with its GPUs for AI training infrastructure. But as AI develops, the larger need is in inference at scale and always-on agentic systems generating myriad tokens—and Nvidia wants to run the entire ecosystem.

This ecosystem is built on both Nvidia's own technology and other providers' technologies. Forbes’ Phoebe Liu talked to Groq cofounder and CEO Jonathan Ross about the specialized AI chip company’s deal with Nvidia, announced last Christmas Eve: $20 billion to license Groq’s product and hire most of its staff. Groq’s chips will be part of the bottom layer of Nvidia’s full-stack platform, integrated with Vera Rubin GPUs.

Nvidia’s revenues have been exponential as the AI boom has remade technology. In just the last three fiscal years alone, they’ve grown about 700%, finishing FY 2026 with a record $215.9 billion in revenues. In his keynote address, Huang forecast $1 trillion in revenue for the current fiscal year, borne by demand for all of Nvidia’s infrastructure.

ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

By now, we know the extreme benefits and drawbacks of OpenClaw: An effective autonomous AI agent that operates on a personal computer and can act on all the data and processes it has, but that broad access also puts personal data at risk. OpenClaw wasn’t designed with security in mind. Some users have come to their computers to find their agents moved private data to places where hackers could easily access it, deleted important files and emails and used malicious code disguised as downloadable “skills” (or “learned” from other agents) to steal information or money.

Docker, an open-source platform that creates isolated computing environments for app development, recently launched its more secure version of the OpenClaw agent called NanoClaw. Creator Gavriel Cohen told Forbes senior contributor Gil Press that the fundamental principle for NanoClaw is creating barriers around agents, meaning they are contained in their own environments and have a limited area in which to take action, stopping bad agent decisions before they become huge problems—and keeping agents from interacting with other agents, which can make problems exponentially worse.

“The right approach isn’t better permission checks or smarter allowlists,” Cohen told Press. “It’s architecture that assumes agents will misbehave and contains the damage when they do.”

Because of its isolation—especially from other agents—NanoClaw is secure and safe by default, Cohen told Forbes senior contributor John Koetsier. Isolation doesn’t mean that NanoClaw can’t do things, though. NanoClaw says it can search the web and do research for you. It can build complex software. It can connect to messaging apps, run scheduled tasks and work with LLMs and other AI platforms. There are also skills to download for NanoClaw, like email reading and management and voice transcription.

While NanoClaw is popular among developers now, with more than 24,300 stars on GitHub to date, it’s part of a still-developing slate of more secure versions of OpenClaw-type agents. Another one—NemoClaw—was among Nvidia’s announcements at GTC 2026 this week.

TECHNOLOGY + INNOVATION

As computer-based AI models continue to improve, developers are also working on AI for the physical world: Robots that can operate machinery, work on assembly lines, perform physical labor in manufacturing facilities and warehouses, and do housework.

But physical AI is different from computer-based AI, which is measured by tokens and massive amounts of compute to process requests. There’s less to see in physical AI at this point—nobody is mass-producing not-quite-ready AI-powered robots—but developers are trying a different approach, writes Forbes senior contributor John Koetsier. Instead of racking up tokens and computing, there are several efforts to “teach” tasks to AI-powered robots in the same way as humans. AI pioneer Yann LeCun recently raised $1 billion for this sort of effort. Before that, researchers at Imperial College London published a paper demonstrating that this approach “taught” AI robots 1,000 different real-world manipulation tasks in a day. Some of the robot training came from a single human demonstration of the task.

Koetsier spoke with Edward Johns, Imperial College’s robotics lab director, about their approach to robot training. It involves breaking actions into several reusable pieces, such as learning to align a robotic hand with an object—a first step in many actions a robot would perform. By separating larger tasks into smaller pieces, the robot reuses prior learning, making it faster to "learn" full actions.

BITS + BYTES

How To Address The Cybersecurity Threats In Your Own System

Cybersecurity strategies are often based on shoring up big systems, which Remedio's CEO, Tal Kollender, compares to locking the front door. But the back door—risks from different systems on top of systems, AI platforms and customizations, and vibe-coded tools—is often wide open. Many companies are unaware of these risks, and their teams’ responsibilities are too distributed to notice.

I spoke to Kollender—a former hacker who is familiar with finding unrealized weaknesses—about how CISOs can identify these vulnerabilities and make their enterprise security stronger. This conversation has been edited for length, clarity and continuity.

Where do you see the biggest cybersecurity vulnerabilities and threats?

Kollender: About 90% of all cybersecurity attacks, once they get in, abuse something called misconfiguration. Misconfiguration can be any kind of default setting or human error or whatnot, but it helps them move laterally.

What we see recently is that people, when they write their own AI agents, don’t think about security first. They think about: Let’s be more efficient. Let’s make sure that this and that works. And they don’t really pay attention. Close to 90% of all organizations adapt AI in one way or another, and they all say, ‘We have our own limitations. No, you don’t have your own limitations if you’re only trying to close [security] on the network level.

AI agents today are so sophisticated because they can create their own code and be malicious without you knowing. You cannot really protect it, and when you find it, it’s already too late.

From the enterprise level, what can a company do to protect against some of these threats?

It is 100% being proactive: not only seeing the risk, but also addressing the risk.

So many vendors say, ‘We have AI just to say we have AI because that’s the new trend.’ We understand it, but we want to stop the AI threat. And it’s not recklessness on purpose. You install OpenClaw or Claude Code and you manage to do pretty much anything, and you don’t understand that the basic configurations when you install it on your device are risky enough that it can go wrong no matter what you do.

It’s not only misconfiguration, but also the remediation on top of that because you need to shrink the attack surface without someone else coming, and it’ll be already too late.

The approach of proactive, autonomous or even auto remediation is something that helps so many customers. They switch a little bit to be in a better position. The security team, along with the infrastructure and the networking teams all need to be one unit and not saying, ‘No, I’m not doing remediation. Let me take you to the other team who is [working] on the remediation.’ They need to have the same agenda to help your organization protect against the next threat act.

What advice would you give to a CISO looking at this landscape and wanting to secure their enterprise?

You have enough visibility tools, you have enough things that will tell you what is wrong, but you don’t have enough actionability. And today, when you see things, you send it to another team to fix it. Usually you don’t get results after that because it’s hard. It’s a lot of manual efforts: processes, scripting, and people are afraid to break stuff. They need to have a proactive approach, and instead of only getting to see alerts, fixing them and working together at the one unit.

COMINGS + GOINGS

  • Software and cloud platform provider ServiceTitan appointed Abhishek Mathur as its chief technology and product officer. Mathur joins the company from Figma, where he worked as senior vice president of software engineering, and has also held leadership roles at Meta and Microsoft.
  • Safety and security solutions firm Pavion selected Keith Ikels as its chief information officer. Ikels joined the company from Pape-Dawson Engineers, where he worked in the same role, as well as Sirius Computer Solutions prior.
  • Cancer diagnostics company Veracyte hired Kevin Haas as its chief development and technology officer, effective March 24. Haas most recently worked as chief technology officer at Myriad Genetics, and fills a newly created leadership role.

STRATEGIES + ADVICE

AI is supposed to help workers do more, but it often leads them to spend more time on less important tasks—partly because of the interactive nature of AI chatbots. Here are ways companies have integrated AI into their operations more effectively, both to get tasks done and boost efficiency.

Today’s leaders know how to handle big displays of disagreement, but they may not be as in tune with smaller ones—eye rolls, sighs, dismissive tones of voice. These smaller gestures still carry weight, and can add up to larger issues on your team. Here’s what to look for, and how to intervene.

QUIZ

Which one of these domains did the federal government appear to register this week?

A. epsteinfiles.gov

B. aliens.gov

C. realnews.gov

D. epicfury.gov

See if you got the answer right here.

item.title: Iran Forces U.S. F-35 Into Emergency Landing—Pilot Survives, Report Says
item.description: The Pentagon did not immediately confirm the cause of the landing, and said the incident was still under investigation.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Folk, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

An F-35 fighter jet flying a combat mission over Iran made an emergency landing at a U.S. airbase in the Middle East, the Pentagon said, due to what sources told CNN they suspect was Iranian fire.

Key Facts

In a statement sent to Forbes, U.S. Central Command spokesperson Captain Tim Hawkins said the military was aware of the reported emergency landing, and confirmed the aircraft landed safely and the pilot was in “stable condition.”

Hawkins said the incident was still under investigation, but did not elaborate on what the military believes caused the landing.

CNN reported the jet was potentially hit by Iranian fire, citing two sources familiar with the incident—which would potentially mark the first time a U.S. military aircraft was hit by Iran since the war began on Feb. 28.

Iran’s Revolutionary Guard took credit for shooting down an F-35, Reuters later reported citing a statement from the group, although the statement also read “the fate of this fighter jet is unknown and is under investigation, and there is a high probability that it crashed."

Key Background

The F-35 Lightning II is a stealth fighter jet built by Lockheed Martin. Both the U.S. and Israel operate these aircraft and are estimated to cost around $100 million, according to CNN.

Crucial Quote

The emergency landing comes only hours after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth backed up the reports that the Pentagon requested another $200 billion to fight the war in Iran. When asked about the reported figure at a press conference on Thursday, Hegseth said, “I think that number could move, obviously. It takes money to kill bad guys. So we’re going back to Congress and focus there to ensure that we’re properly funded.”

Tangent

Last week, a U.S. KC-135 refueling plane crashed in Iraq, killing all six crew members onboard. An Iran-aligned Iraqi group took credit for shooting the aircraft down, but the Pentagon denied the crash was caused by either enemy or friendly fire. Earlier in the conflict, two F-15 Strike Eagle fighter jets were shot down by Kuwaiti air defenses in an apparent friendly fire incident. The pilots of both aircraft safely ejected.

item.title: Northern Lights Update: 18 States Still In Aurora Borealis Viewline Thursday
item.description: A geomagnetic storm is upping the visibility of the aurora borealis.
item.dc:creator: Mary Whitfill Roeloffs, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

The aurora borealis could light up skies into the middle United States Thursday and Friday nights thanks to a geomagnetic storm that will push the Kp index to six and extend the visibility of the northern lights further south than usual.

Key Facts

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration has predicted geomagnetic storms to arrive from Thursday to Sunday spurred by the arrival of multiple coronal mass ejections, or eruptions of plasma and magnetic fields from the sun's outer atmosphere.

The storms could also be fueled by a negative polarity coronal hole high-speed stream headed toward Earth, NOAA says, which compresses the coronal mass ejection's plasma and magnetic field and results in enhanced geomagnetic activity on Earth—and a more visible aurora.

NOAA forecast a Kp index of six on a scale of nine, reflecting a moderate geomagnetic storm, overnight Thursday into the early hours of Friday and again at around midnight ET Saturday.

Get Forbes Breaking News Text Alerts: We’re launching text message alerts so you'll always know the biggest stories shaping the day’s headlines. Text “Alerts” to (201) 335-0739 or sign up here: joinsubtext.com/forbes.

Which States Will See Northern Lights Thursday Night?

NOAA’s predicted “view line” for Tuesday encompassed all of Alaska, Montana, North Dakota, South Dakota, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Michigan and Maine. Essentially all of Washington, Vermont and New Hampshire are also forecast to see the lights, as are northern and western New York and northern portions of Nebraska, Oregon, Idaho, Wyoming, Iowa and Illinois (see graphic below).

What’s The Best Way To See The Northern Lights?

The best chance of seeing the lights usually comes between between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. local time, according to NOAA. Hopeful viewers should travel as far north as possible to a high, north-facing vantage point away from light pollution.

How Should The Northern Lights Be Photographed?

Those using an iPhone should use night mode and those with an Android should use the device’s Pro mode. All smartphone photographers should disable flash and shoot in RAW image format. Those using a separate camera could set the aperture or F-stop to four or less and use a focus set to the furthest possible setting. All photographers should use a tripod for stabilizing the image.

item.title: Senate Committee Approves Mullin’s Nomination As DHS Chief
item.description: Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is stepping down at the end of the month following a string of controversies.
item.dc:creator: Sara Dorn, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

The Senate Homeland Security Committee advanced Sen. Markwayne Mullin’s, R-Okla., nomination to lead the Department of Homeland Security when Kristi Noem steps down at the end of the month, making his confirmation before the full Senate all but certain.

Key Facts

The committee approved Mullin in an 8-7 vote Thursday, with Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., voting with Republicans and Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky., voting with Democrats.

The full Senate is likely to vote on his confirmation by early next week, Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., told Politico.

President Donald Trump announced earlier this month he was firing Noem and nominating Mullin to replace her following intense criticism of a $220 million ad campaign that prominently featured her and benefited Republican operatives.

The Senate committee voted on Mullin’s nomination a day after he clashed with Paul in his confirmation hearing over what Paul described as a “pattern” of aggressive behavior and was questioned by Democrats over whether he would continue the Trump administration’s hardline approach to immigration.

Mullin, a member of the Cherokee nation who served in the House before he was elected to the Senate in 2023, supports Trump’s hardline immigration policies and defended the shootings of two Americans in Minneapolis earlier this year by federal immigration officials.

Tangent

Paul criticized Mullin for saying he “understands completely” why Paul’s neighbor attacked him in 2017. “Tell it to my face, tell the world why you believe I deserved to be assaulted from behind, have six ribs broken and a damaged lung,” Paul said, adding “while you’re at it, explain to the American public why they should trust a man with anger issues” to be DHS secretary. Mullin acknowledged the comments he made about Paul, but did not apologize, telling the committee, “everybody in this room knows that I’m very blunt and direct to the point, and if I have something to say, I’ll say it directly to your face.”

Key Background

Noem was fired days after she faced intense grilling from lawmakers of both parties about her leadership style in a public congressional hearing. Noem claimed Trump approved the controversial $220 million ad campaign, though Trump later told NBC he “didn’t know about it” and “wasn’t thrilled,” though he denied it prompted her firing. Some Democrats are seeking criminal charges against Noem for her statements in the hearing, accusing her of lying under oath, including by saying Trump approved her ad campaign. Noem was also heavily criticized for blaming Alex Pretti and Renee Good for their shootings by immigration officials in Minneapolis earlier this year. She’s also been accused of financial mismanagement, including delaying approval of some key emergency aid contracts by implementing a policy that requires her to sign off on contracts more than $100,000. In the wake of the backlash over the killings, the Trump administration has rolled back some of its aggressive immigration enforcement tactics, including conducting fewer street sweeps and instead focusing on more targeted raids, leading to a dip in arrests, The New York Times reported. Mullin did not say whether he would make changes to some of Trump’s controversial immigration enforcement policies in Wednesday’s hearing, but he committed to getting rid of Noem’s contract approval process and vowed to keep the Federal Emergency Management Agency intact, despite Noem saying she wanted the agency eliminated.

Further Reading

Markwayne Mullin Slammed For ‘Anger Issues’ In Testy Confirmation Hearing (Forbes)

Kristi Noem Being Ousted As DHS Secretary (Forbes)

Nearly 60% Of Voters Think Kristi Noem Should Be Removed After Minnesota Shootings (Forbes)

item.title: ‘It Takes Money To Kill Bad Guys’: Hegseth Doesn’t Deny Pentagon’s $200 Billion Iran War Funding Request
item.description: The war cost more than $11 billion in the first week alone, Pentagon officials reportedly told lawmakers.
item.dc:creator: Sara Dorn, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth didn’t deny Thursday that the Pentagon is seeking $200 billion in additional funding for the war in Iran, telling lawmakers the figure “could move,” teeing up a potential partisan battle in Congress as the war has become increasingly complex and politically risky for President Donald Trump.

Key Facts

Hegseth said, “I think that number could move, obviously,” when asked by a reporter about the report for the funding request in The Washington Post a day earlier, adding, “it takes money to kill bad guys.”

The Pentagon has reportedly asked the White House to seek $200 billion from Congress to fund the war, unnamed sources told The Washington Post.

The war cost more than $11 billion in weaponry in the first week alone, not including the cost of personnel and other expenses, Pentagon officials told lawmakers in a recent briefing, according to multiple reports.

Comparatively, Congress has approved $188 billion in total, as of December, to help fund Ukraine’s war with Russia.

Hegseth also said Thursday, “today will be the largest strike package yet,” vowing “death and destruction from above.”

Hegseth refused to say how long the war could last and at what point would the U.S. determine it’s achieved its goals, telling reporters, “we wouldn’t want to set a definitive time frame on that, but as we’ve said, we’re on plan,” adding, “it will be at the president’s choosing, ultimately, where we say ‘hey we’ve achieved what we need to on behalf of the American people to ensure our security.’”

What To Watch For

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Thursday the U.S. could lift sanctions on Iranian oil that is already at sea and the U.S. could release some strategic oil reserves in an effort to mitigate surging global oil prices. Attacks by Iran and Israel have severely damaged key oil facilities in the Persian Gulf in recent days. U.S. gas prices rose again Thursday, to $3.89, up 32.5% from the previous month.

Crucial Quote

“They came into this fight with a lot of weapons; this is why we continue to be as aggressive and assertive as we can against their ballistic missile capability . . . we are continuing to hunt and find them and kill them and we will continue to do so, so they still retain some capability,” Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman Gen. Dan Caine said Thursday.

Key Background

Attacks on oil facilities in Qatar and Saudi Arabia have raised concerns in recent days the war could spiral into a wider regional conflict. Trump on Wednesday distanced the U.S. from Israeli attacks on the South Pars gas field in Iran, and said the attack was carried out by Israel without U.S. assistance. He vowed Israel would not carry out any more strikes on the field unless Iran attacks Qatar, in which case, he said, the U.S. “will massively blow up the entirety of the South Pars Gas Field.” Meanwhile, the Strait of Hormuz, a key oil passage, is still effectively at a standstill, as U.S. allies have refused Trump’s request for assistance in escorting tankers through the waterway.

Further Reading

Oil Hits $115 As Qatar Says Iran Caused ‘Extensive Damage’ To Energy Hub (Forbes)

JD Vance Makes Rare Remark On Iran War: Says Trump Won’t ‘Risk’ A ‘Long-Term’ Conflict (Forbes)

Trump: ‘Most’ NATO Allies Say They’ll Stay Out Of Iran War (Forbes)

item.title: Dow, S&P 500 And Nasdaq Fall To 2026 Low: Inflation Fears And Oil Rattle Markets
item.description: The Fed said the Iran war’s impact on the U.S. is “uncertain,” as oil prices briefly neared $120.
item.dc:creator: Ty Roush, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

Each market index stumbled to its lowest level this year as trading opened on Thursday, with a further selloff fueled by a hotter-than-expected inflation report and surging energy prices amid the Iran war, as oil briefly neared $120 earlier in the day.

Key Facts

The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped by more than 270 points, or nearly 0.6%, shortly after trading opened, while the Nasdaq and S&P 500 slipped by 0.9% and 0.8%, respectively, paring back earlier losses after each index hit its lowest point so far this year.

A decline on Thursday pushed the S&P 500 below its 200-day moving average—used to track the long-term trend of an equity—for the first time since May 2025, joining the Dow, which closed below its 200-day moving average on Wednesday, suggesting both indexes were trending toward a further decline.

Boeing (down 3.4%) headlined losses across the 30-stock Dow, followed by Sherwin-Williams (1.8%), Caterpillar (1.6%) and Nvidia (1.6%), with further declines across the tech-heavy Nasdaq from Tesla (2.5%), Amazon (1.2%), Meta (1%) and Alphabet (1%).

Brent Crude, the international oil benchmark, rose to around $111.15 as of Thursday morning after earlier rising as high as $119.11, nearing its highest point since hitting $119.50 earlier this month.

Why Are Stocks Falling Today?

Another rout across the broader stock market followed worse-than-expected readings on the U.S. economy on Wednesday. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported wholesale inflation accelerated in February, with wholesale prices rising 3.4% annually and 0.7% from January to February, surpassing analyst estimates of 2.9% and 0.3%, respectively, according to FactSet. That preceded a vote from the Federal Reserve to hold interest rates between 3.5% and 3.75%, and the path toward future cuts remained unclear after Fed Chair Jerome Powell signaled “you won’t see a rate cut” unless inflation improved. The Fed’s “dot plot,” a graph outlining the central bank’s policymaking expectations, still indicated they expected one interest rate cut in 2026. Powell said it was “too soon” to assess the economic impact of the Iran war, including whether surging oil prices would further boost inflation.

Key Background

Accelerating oil prices have disrupted equities in the weeks after the U.S. and Israel struck Iran. Those attacks, including threats from U.S. and Iranian officials, have stoked fears of the global oil supply being disrupted and then raising and prolonging inflation in the U.S. One Iranian military official warned oil prices could reach $200 per barrel, while Qatar’s energy minister claimed oil could top out at $150 per barrel if the conflict persists. Several economists also discussed whether the rising oil prices would bring on a recession, including Moody’s chief economist Mark Zandi, who noted every recession since World War II—except during the pandemic—was preceded by surging oil costs. Other analysts noted oil prices would need to settle well above their all-time high of $147 to disrupt the U.S. economy.

Further Reading

MORE FROM FORBESFed Holds Interest Rates Steady—Warns Iran War May Have ‘Uncertain’ ImpactBy Ty Roush

item.title: Oil Hits $115 As Qatar Says Iran Caused ‘Extensive Damage’ To Energy Hub
item.description: The surge in global crude oil prices continued on Thursday after Qatar reported “extensive damage” from an Iranian attack on its energy infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Siladitya Ray, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Topline

Global crude oil prices surged more than 6% early on Thursday, a day after Iran retaliated against an Israeli strike on its biggest gas field by attacking neighboring Qatar’s main energy hub and causing “extensive damage,” as the U.S.-Israel war against Iran continued to disrupt energy infrastructure in the region.

Key Facts

The global benchmark, Brent Crude index, touched $116.13 per barrel early on Thursday, up more than 8% from the previous day.

After hovering under the $105 mark for most of the week, the global benchmark touched $110 on Wednesday after President Donald Trump suggested that the U.S. military could ‘finish off’ Iran, leaving other countries to deal with the aftermath and secure the Strait of Hormuz.

The index spiked further early on Thursday after Qatar’s state-run energy giant declared that Iran’s attack on its energy infrastructure had caused “extensive damage.”

Saudi Arabia’s defense ministry said it had intercepted a missile over the Red Sea port city of Yanbu and a drone fell into a refinery in the city, a potential sign that Iran may be expanding its attacks.

The U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate remained a bit more muted, touching $97.24 per barrel—up 1.05% compared to Wednesday.

What About U.s. Gasoline Prices?

The national average gas price rose once again on Thursday, touching $3.89, according to AAA’s fuel price tracker, a 32.5% increase from the previous month. The national average price of Diesel nearly reached $5.10 early on Thursday, just two days after breaching $5. Diesel is used to fuel heavy vehicles like trucks, tractors, combines, and construction machinery and a spike in its price is likely to impact the whole economy. Everything from groceries to shipping prices are expected to rise as a result.

What Has Qatar Said About Iran’s Attack?

In response to Israeli strikes on its oil and gas infrastructure, Iran launched attacks against U.S. allied Gulf countries on Wednesday. Qatar’s main energy hub, the Ras Laffan Industrial City, was the target of an Iranian retaliatory strike and the state-owned firm QatarEnergy said in a statement, that the missile attacks have resulted in fires causing “extensive damage.” QatarEnergy said several of its Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) facilities were also targeted by missiles that caused “sizeable fires and extensive further damage.”

What About The Attacks On Saudi Arabia?

In a potential sign that Iran was expanding its retaliation to target oil infrastructure on the Red Sea coast Saudi Arabia said a drone had fallen into the SAMREF oil refinery and a damage assessment was ongoing. Saudi Arabia has used the Red Sea route to transport crude oil as its eastern coast in the Persian Gulf remains blockaded by Iran. So an expansion of the strikes to target facilities along the Saudi western coast could further disrupt global oil shipments.

Further Reading

Trump Threatens To ‘Blow Up’ Iran’s Biggest Gas Field If It Continues Striking Qatar (Forbes)

item.title: Judge Blocks RFK Jr.’s Vaccine Agenda
item.description: In this week’s edition of InnovationRx, we look at Roche’s big AI drug development bet, an E. coli outbreak in raw milk cheddar, R1 Therapeutics’ $78 million raise, and more.
item.dc:creator: Amy Feldman, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
In this week’s edition of InnovationRx, we look at the legal ruling against RFK Jr.’s vaccine agenda, Roche’s big AI drug development bet, an E. coli outbreak in raw milk cheddar, R1 Therapeutics’ $78 million raise, and more. To get it in your inbox, subscribe here.

A federal judge in Boston ruled that Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s efforts to overhaul vaccine policy in the United States were likely illegal.

The preliminary ruling from Judge Brian E. Murphy said that Kennedy’s remake of the vaccine advisory panel and his changes to the childhood vaccine schedule violated the Administrative Procedure Act, which governs how federal agencies put into effect policy changes.

The ruling isn’t final, as the Trump Administration can, and likely will, appeal and the lawsuit may still proceed to trial. However, it is a win for the American Academy of Pediatrics and other medical groups that had accused the Trump Administration of acting unlawfully in its efforts to overhaul vaccine policy. That’s because these types of rulings are only typically granted if a judge thinks a party will succeed.

“This is a significant victory for public health, evidence-based medicine, the rule of law, and the American people,” Richard Hughes, an attorney for the medical groups, said in a statement cited by USA Today.

The decision halts Kennedy’s order, announced in January, to end broad recommendations for children to be vaccinated against diseases that include RSV, hepatitis A and B, flu, Covid and rotavirus vaccine. While the CDC’s recommendations are not legally binding, states and doctors typically follow them, and they can affect insurance coverage.

The Trump Administration’s efforts to cut back on recommended vaccinations comes at a time when preventable diseases have been spreading, particularly among unvaccinated children. For example, the CDC reports there have been more than 1,360 cases of measles so far this year–more than half the number reported in all of 2025.

The injunction also postpones the next meeting of the overhauled vaccine advisory panel, which had been scheduled for this week. Kennedy, who had been an anti-vaccine activist before becoming HHS Secretary, had purged the committee and brought in his own members last year.

Roche Launches New ‘AI Factory’ To Develop Drugs Faster

Pharmaceutical giant Roche said on Monday that it is launching a new large-scale “AI factory” with thousands of the latest Nvidia chips to accelerate the development of new drugs and diagnostics.

Roche said the company has ramped up its AI capacity by purchasing 2,176 Nvidia Blackwell GPUs, which will be deployed across the U.S. and Europe. That brings its total GPUs to more than 3,500, which it claims is the greatest number owned by any pharmaceutical company. Roche, which made the announcement in conjunction with Nvidia’s GTC conference in San Jose, hopes to use that technological firepower to discover, develop, manufacture and commercialize therapies faster.

While Roche, which has a market cap of $340 billion, is based in Switzerland, its U.S. subsidiary Genentech is well known for developing drugs to treat cancer and immune system diseases, including Herceptin (for breast cancer) and Avastin (for colon and lung cancer). Roche brought in $78 billion in revenue last year at current exchange rates.

The company declined to disclose how much it is spending on this effort, but it is “a substantial sum,” Aviv Regev, executive vice president and head of Genentech research and early development, tells Forbes. “It does highlight how important AI is for our business.” Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has said its Blackwell chips cost between $30,000 and $40,000 a pop, which would place the company’s investment at $65 million or more for the chips.

Read more here.

FDA Investigating An E. Coli Outbreak Linked To Raw Milk Cheddar Cheese

The FDA is investigating an outbreak of E. coli linked to Raw Farm brand cheddar cheese, which is made with raw milk. The FDA recommended that Raw Farm voluntarily recall their raw milk cheeses from the market, but the company declined to do so.

Forbes profiled California’s Raw Farm in December 2024 when the family farm – the country’s largest producer of raw milk – was doubling down on its production amid a bird flu state of emergency. “I’m the raw milk guy,” cofounder Mark McAfee said then. “I’m the only guy that knows this stuff.”

Raw milk, which has recently been promoted by the MAHA movement, can harbor a number of pathogens, including bacteria and viruses, that can make people ill to the point of hospitalization or death. Ever since French scientist Louis Pasteur invented pasteurization – which heats milk to at least 145 degrees Fahrenheit to kill harmful bacteria – back in 1862, that’s been the standard in America.

Deal of the Week

Bay Area-startup R1 Therapeutics emerged from stealth with $78 million in funding and a license for a new therapy for chronic kidney disease from China-based Alebund Therapeutics. R1 plans to start mid-stage clinical trials on the treatment this year. The funding round was led by Carlyle’s Abingworth, DaVita Venture Group and F-Prime. Dr. Krishna Polu, the company’s cofounder and CEO, is a nephrologist who has worked as an advisor to Abingworth.

WHAT WE’RE READING

More people are buying ketamine online, but with little regulation or oversight, some who use the psychedelic-like anesthetic are dying.

How compounders of GLP-1 weight loss drugs helped pressure Lilly and Novo to cut prices and reshaped the industry.

The Wall Street Journal investigates how the boom in autism therapy became Medicaid’s fastest-growing jackpot–with payments as high as $800 an hour.

Nevada regulators issued fines of up to $10,000 for peptide providers at an anti-aging festival where two women became critically ill.

The White House lifted a hold that prevented the NIH from spending money on medical research.

MORE FROM FORBES

MORE FROM FORBESThis Argentine Billionaire’s Startup Vercel Is One Of Claude Code’s Go-To Web Hosting ToolsBy Richard Nieva
MORE FROM FORBESInside The World’s Richest Shipper Gianluigi Aponte’s Apparent Bid To Corner The Supertanker MarketBy Giacomo Tognini
MORE FROM FORBESWhy An Unsustainable Bubble Is Growing Inside FintechBy Jeff Kauflin

item.title: Malaysian Royals Step Up Telecoms, Property Ventures
item.description: Malaysian King Sultan Ibrahim Iskandar's daughter, Tun Aminah, was appointed U Mobile and Berjaya Property chairman, while his son, Ismail, seeks to build luxury bungalows on the family’s prime heritage property in Singapore.
item.dc:creator: Ian Sayson, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
The children of Malaysian King Sultan Ibrahim Iskandar are stepping up their business ventures in telecommunications and real estate, tapping on the royal family’s vast resources.

The king’s daughter, Tun Aminah Sultan Ibrahim Ismail, has been named chairman of U Mobile and Berjaya Property, according to regulatory filings from Berjaya Corp., the Malaysian conglomerate founded by tycoon Vincent Tan.

Tun Aminah’s appointment took effect March 13 as Tan stepped down, U Mobile said in a statement late Tuesday. The leadership change comes after Mawar Setia—an investment holding company founded by Aminah and Tan—raised its stake in U Mobile above 50% following the acquisition last year of additional shares from ST Telemedia, part of Singapore state-linked investment company Temasek.

“The latest changes in the U Mobile boardroom reflect the company’s commitment to building digital infrastructure that is reliable, scalable and delivered on time,” Tan said in the statement. U Mobile is one of two telcos awarded the 5G license by the Malaysian government.

Tun Aminah’s appointments comes as her brother, Ismail, seeks to build luxury bungalows on the family’s prime property in Singapore, one of the world’s most expensive real estate markets.

Sultan Ibrahim has extensive business interests including stakes in Berjaya Assets, the developer of the Berjaya Times Square in Kuala Lumpur, and U Mobile. The royal family owns huge tracts of land in the Iskandar region in the southern Malaysian state of Johor, an area which Malaysia and Singapore are jointly developing as a special economic zone.

The king’s son, Ismail, is the Regent of Johor (ROJ), which oversees the development of his family’s heritage properties. In June last year, he agreed to exchange 13 hectares of his family’s land close to the Singapore Botanic Gardens, a UNESCO heritage site, for a nearby 8.5-hectare state-owned land parcel of equivalent value, according to the Urban Redevelopment Authority. The swap paves the way for development of the property.

“The ROJ has now submitted a development application to develop his land for low-rise, low-density housing with a gross plot ratio of 1.4 as well as good class bungalows,” URA said in a statement on Tuesday.

The property in the Tyersall Avenue area has been held by the family for more than a century. Following the completion of the swap, the king’s family will own about 16.6 hectares of undeveloped land, which analysts estimate could be worth at least S$3.6 billion ($2.8 billion).

item.title: Forbes House of the Week: Tropical Modern
item.description: Houses from Forbes “America's Best Architects” 2025 lists
item.dc:creator: Richard Olsen, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
ESSENTIALS

Firm Name: Seibert Architects

Principal: Michael Epstein

Headquarters: 1373 Fifth Street, Sarasota, Florida

Accolades: Forbes Architecture’s “America’s Top 200 Residential Architects,” 2025; Forbes Architecture’s “America’s Best-in-State Residential Architects,” 2025.

House Name: Mhouse South

Location: Sarasota, Florida

Area & Layout: 2,560 square feet, 2BR, 4BA; 512 square feet, studio/office

Architectural Photographer: Ryan Gamma (@rgammaphoto)

“Architects building in relatively warm climates have a special responsibility and contribution to make... The Deep South, because of its relatively warm climate, its impressive architectural heritage and its deep love of the good life, can contribute a unique quality.”

—Paul Rudolph (1918–1997), Architect

Oh, to be on the radiant quartz beaches of Sarasota County, Florida, in the blue-skies-glorious late-winter to spring months. Or, more precisely, to maximize that experience by retreating at day’s end to a beachhouse of one’s very own. But what kind of house would it be—rather, for this particular stretch of the Gulf Coast, what should it be, experientially and as an investment?

For the architect of houses, Florida’s Gulf Coast is among the most demanding and risk-prone of climatic settings in all of the U.S., requiring as it does an extremist’s emphasis on the building’s own “self-awareness” and intelligence factor, especially in relation to sun control and flooding and stormwater management. Architect Michael Epstein—who has practiced here since joining, in the 1980s, the late Sarasota School legend Tim Seibert’s celebrated firm, Seibert Architects—brings an exceptional, if not incomparable, degree of discernment to the challenge.

But what makes this architect’s work worthy of national attention and contributed to the firm’s landing on the list of Forbes Top Residential Architects in America 2025, is how a Seibert Architects house rises to the elevated occasion of high-art architecture. And how it does so while also managing—as too few houses do—to be both “smart” and “comfortable” simultaneously. Of course, photography can’t possibly capture the complexities of that whole story. But fortunately, in this instance, it comes close.

RICHARD OLSEN, Forbes Senior Editor, Architecture: In terms of scale, scope and identity, how does this project fit into your overall body of residential work?

MICHAEL EPSTEIN: Our designs continue to be rooted in the regional Modernist work that our firm, along with a handful of others, became famous for in the mid-century era, known as the Sarasota School of Design—Modern designs suited for the Florida coastal climate, now often referred to as Tropical Modern. We have designed many projects which are substantially smaller, some larger, and some remodeling or additions to existing residences. Whether a new house or modifications to an existing house, it is not the size of the project that attracts us, but the promise of a meaningful end result. Even though, true to the firm’s Modernist roots, we work diligently to ensure that the end result captures the spirit of the owners and responds to its immediate context. As a result, the look of the houses that we design can vary considerably from one to the next.

OLSEN: Creatively, from a design problem-solving viewpoint, what are a few of the most satisfying solutions that came together here?

MICHAEL EPSTEIN: The decision to arrange the spaces within the house around an elevated courtyard created a captured outdoor space that maintainins privacy and allows interior spaces and the courtyard to become a part of each other, both visually and functionally. The circular hole at the entry porch and in the roof overhang in the courtyard, as well as the floating circular mirrors in the master bath, provide a needed counterpoint to the rectilinear forms of the house and soften the overall effect of the house, just enough. While a small detail, the shower seats do not touch the surrounding walls, and appear to have no support or attachment, giving the illusion that they hover in place. Makes me smile every time I see them.

OLSEN: And what’s next for the studio?

MICHAEL EPSTEIN: We will continue to focus on solutions that maximize the benefit of what the site has to offer and can enrich the lives of occupants. Since Mhouse, we completed another large residence, on a heavily vegetated site, which takes advantage of the surrounding vegetation in a variety of ways. We are currently working on a new design for a large house that is focused on capturing both near and distant views of the bay. In contrast to these, we are also in progress on a very small house nestled into a natural setting, which is open to its surroundings and exposes its structure and the materials of its construction both inside and out.


More from Forbes House of the Week

item.title: Forbes House of the Week: Big Sky Country
item.description: Houses from Forbes “America's Best Architects” 2025 lists
item.dc:creator: Richard Olsen, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
ESSENTIALS

Firm Name: Minarik Architecture

Principal: Doug Minarik

Headquarters: 618 North Wallace, Bozeman, Montana

Accolades: Forbes Architecture’s “America’s Top 200 Residential Architects,” 2025; Forbes Architecture’s “America’s Best-in-State Residential Architects,” 2025.

House Name: Bridger Canyon

Location: Bozeman, Montana

Area & Layout: 2,622 square feet, 2BR, 3BA; 1,026 square feet, detached garage and studio

Architectural Photographer: Audrey Hall (audreyhall.com)

“Space holds its own internal mystery, from the cathedrals of enormous wild places to a small, cozy, and deeply satisfying room. The beauty of space evolves only by being defined and enclosed... In mountainous, western Montana, huge valleys are vividly defined by their surrounding mountains, and most amazing is how even the sky is defined by mountains.”

—Daphne Bugbee Jones (1921–2012), Pioneering Montana Modernist Architect

Into the viewshed of Montana’s Bridger Range we go in this installment of Forbes House of the Week, with Bozeman’s Minarik Architecture as our lead. A Forbes Top 200 Residential Architects 2025 listee and an integral part of a Montana contemporary architecture scene that continues to advance the state’s 75-year tradition of Modernist home design, Doug Minarik, firm principal, is as much “editor” as he is form-maker. In his houses, each of them site specific, that which can be deemed non-essential tends to be summarily ruled out. It’s a guiding principle that allows for a smaller, quieter and, on the whole, more thoughtful outcome for his clients.

RICHARD OLSEN, Forbes Senior Editor, Architecture: In terms of scale, scope, and identity, how does this project fit into your overall body of residential work?

DOUG MINARIK: This home is a great example of our approach to residential design. That said, we are constantly evolving our approach to how spaces are scaled for livability. In this instance, the home is scaled for two people but can flex and accommodate visits from their adult children’s families and friends. There is a calming effect to editing back unnecessary architectural language and allowing a space to simply and modestly support day-to-day living. The architecture is static, creating a backdrop that is poised to celebrate the changing landscape and support the pulse of the day’s activity. Too much architecture can muddy that experience.

OLSEN: Creatively, from a design problem-solving viewpoint, what are a few of the most satisfying solutions that came together here?

DOUG MINARIK: The views to the north are stunning and in a state of constant change with weather and seasons. We were cognizant of making sure the experience of the landscape did not become routine. We challenged ourselves to use a singular roofline as an organizing element, under which subtle changes in floor level and orientation frame the same landscape in different ways. When moving between spaces, the big north views are momentarily removed and scale is reduced to a more intimate experience with southern light filtered through the trees and into the wood-lined corridor.

OLSEN: And what’s next for the studio?

DOUG MINARIK: We continue to focus on homes that prioritize longevity and a carefully considered relationship with their surroundings. This has led to a number of recent projects throughout the northwest where we are able to focus the design decisions on quality and scale of space, tactile details, and building-envelope solutions that reduce energy usage and increase comfort. All of the behind-the-scenes effort translates into simply enjoying a cup of coffee in a correctly sized mug on a small porch oriented to catch the morning sun.


More from Forbes House of the Week

item.title: Forbes House of the Week: New England Renewal
item.description: Houses from Forbes “America's Best Architects” 2025 lists
item.dc:creator: Richard Olsen, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
ESSENTIALS

Firm Name: Haver & Skolnick Architects

Principals: Charles M. Haver, Stewart R. Skolnick

Headquarters: 400A Heritage Village, Southbury, Connecticut

Accolades: Forbes Architecture’s “America’s Top 200 Residential Architects,” 2025; Forbes Architecture’s “America’s Best-in-State Residential Architects,” 2025.

House Name: Saltbox Farm

Location: The Northwestern Hills region of Connecticut, a 150-acre working farm

Area & Layout: Original house, 2,000 square feet; Addition, 7,800 square feet: 6 BR, 5 BA, 3 Half-baths

“As in the child’s sketch, the Cape Cod cottage says ‘house.’ While the classic Cape was indigenous to the sand dunes of southern New England...it gained acceptance across America. Maybe more important than its practicality, the Cape is a truly democratic form, a house for all people in this nation of immigrants.”

—William Morgan (b. 1944), Forbes Architecture Advisor and Author of The Cape Cod Cottage

ith this installment of Forbes House of the Week we debut a new approach: yielding the floor to the architects themselves. In this, our ninth installment, we continue to explore best practices in American homebuilding through a recently completed restoration/addition by Connecticut’s Haver & Skolnick Architects. The firm, one of Forbes’ Top 200 Residential Architects in America for 2025, is known for its expertise in the New England vernacular. Here, they again bring our collective past to the fore—in a thoroughly modern way.

RICHARD OLSEN, Forbes Senior Editor, Architecture: In terms of scale, scope, and identity, how does this project fit into your overall body of residential work?

CHARLES HAVER AND STEWART SKOLNICK: This is the type of project we’re best known for, creating comfortable, rambling rural retreats that appear to have evolved over time. In this case, we had the bonus of restoring and drawing design inspiration from a true piece of history, a 1770 saltbox house. We especially like the challenge of working with a large property, creating exterior rooms, as with the series of outdoor living and entertaining spaces on the east side of the home. We also enjoyed working with a variety of specialty spaces: a gym, pub, media room and our first golf-simulator room.

OLSEN: Creatively, from a design problem-solving viewpoint, what are a few of the most satisfying solutions that came together here?

HAVER AND SKOLNICK: By far, the greatest design challenge of this project was balancing the large scale of the addition with the diminutive scale of the antique house. The owners’ extensive program required an addition nearly four times the size of the original house, with a specific request for spacious rooms with tall ceilings. We quickly realized that a conventional approach of adding to either side of the house would overwhelm the saltbox. This led to the concept of designing the addition as a backdrop to the antique house, only attached with a narrow connection at the rear. This approach showcases the distinctive saltbox form of the antique house and diminishes the impact of the much larger addition.

OLSEN: And what’s next for the studio?

HAVER AND SKOLNICK: We look forward to continuing to create large-scale rural retreats for our clients, inspired by local historic buildings and landscapes. We’re also enjoying assisting past clients with updating their homes and properties to suit their current needs. The recent construction of our own weekend home on Fishers Island, New York, gave us a taste for designing in a seaside aesthetic, which is very appealing. To pursue this further, we’re establishing a satellite office on Fishers Island to better serve the coastal communities of New England and New York.


More from Forbes House of the Week
